summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that SaaS companies drastically undercharge by using linear per-unit pricing when customer value scales exponentially with usage. Using Server Density as a case study, he shows how switching from per-server variable pricing to a tiered plan model ($99/$299/$499) doubled revenue per visitor cohort. The old $11/server model attracted price-sensitive hobbyists who generated disproportionate support costs, while the new tiers segmented customers by business seriousness. He emphasizes that most customers don't care about price, and those who do are disproportionately terrible customers. The piece advocates for radical pricing restructurings over incremental tweaks.
key_points: Linear per-unit pricing fundamentally misaligns with customer value: a 7-server company gets 50x the value of a 1-server company, not 7x, yet pays only 7x more
Switching from variable per-server pricing to 3-4 tiered plans doubled Server Density's monthly revenue per visitor cohort despite reducing free trial signups by 25%
Low price points attract pathological customers who value their own time at zero, generate excessive support load, and complain the most
Tiered pricing is easier to buy (fewer decisions), more predictable, and better aligned with business goals than configurable per-unit pricing
Price testing with radical packaging restructurings moves the needle far more than tweaking plan prices by $10
money_quotes: You don't get 7x the value from server monitoring if you increase your server fleet by 7x, you get (at least) 50x the value.
Most customers do not care about price. Those that do are disproportionately terrible customers.
They'll complain that Server Density costs infinity percent more than OSS, because they value their own time at zero.
At this point, there's someone in the audience saying 'That's illegal!' That person is just plain wrong. There is no carbon in a water molecule, and price testing is not illegal.
Serious businesses generate a lot of money no matter how many servers they have.
Virtually no one gets pricing right on the first try.
The variable pricing customers largely a) don't want to talk about things and b) need a password reset right now WTF is taking so long.

Are $13 accounts for server monitoring likely to be experienced sysadmins doing meaningful work for businesses who will solve their own problems and pay without complaint every month? No, they're going to be the worst possible pathological customers.
key_insight: SaaS pricing should scale with customer value, not usage units — switching from linear per-unit pricing to tiered plans segments away pathological customers and captures the exponential value serious businesses derive from your product.
